web.description: How to use Spec Kit in your agentic workflows. Spec Kit works with coding agents like GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI. The key is to ...
web.markdown:
[Den Delimarsky](https://github.blog/author/localden/ "Posts by Den Delimarsky")· [@localden](https://github.com/localden)

September 2, 2025

\|
9 minutes

- Share:
- [Share on X](https://x.com/share?text=Spec-driven%20development%20with%20AI%3A%20Get%20started%20with%20a%20new%20open%20source%20toolkit&url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.blog%2Fai-and-ml%2Fgenerative-ai%2Fspec-driven-development-with-ai-get-started-with-a-new-open-source-toolkit%2F)
- [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?t=Spec-driven%20development%20with%20AI%3A%20Get%20started%20with%20a%20new%20open%20source%20toolkit&u=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.blog%2Fai-and-ml%2Fgenerative-ai%2Fspec-driven-development-with-ai-get-started-with-a-new-open-source-toolkit%2F)
- [Share on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?title=Spec-driven%20development%20with%20AI%3A%20Get%20started%20with%20a%20new%20open%20source%20toolkit&url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.blog%2Fai-and-ml%2Fgenerative-ai%2Fspec-driven-development-with-ai-get-started-with-a-new-open-source-toolkit%2F)

As coding agents have grown more powerful, a pattern has emerged: you describe your goal, get a block of code back, and often… it looks right, but doesn’t quite work. This “vibe-coding” approach can be great for quick prototypes, but less reliable when building serious, mission-critical applications or working with existing codebases.

Sometimes the code doesn’t compile. Sometimes it solves part of the problem but misses the actual intent. The stack or architecture may not be what you’d choose.

The issue isn’t the coding agent’s coding ability, but our approach. We treat coding agents like search engines when we should be treating them more like literal-minded pair programmers. They excel at pattern recognition but still need unambiguous instructions.

That’s why we’re rethinking specifications — not as static documents, but as living, executable artifacts that evolve with the project. Specs become the shared source of truth. When something doesn’t make sense, you go back to the spec; when a project grows complex, you refine it; when tasks feel too large, you break them down.

Spec Kit, our new open sourced toolkit for spec-driven development, provides a structured process to bring spec-driven development to your coding agent workflows with tools including GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI.

## What is the spec-driven process with Spec Kit?

[Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit) makes your specification the center of your engineering process. Instead of writing a spec and setting it aside, the spec drives the implementation, checklists, and task breakdowns.  Your primary role is to steer; the coding agent does the bulk of the writing.

It works in four phases with clear checkpoints. But here’s the key insight: each phase has a specific job, and you don’t move to the next one until the current task is fully validated.

Here’s how the process breaks down:

1. **Specify**: You provide a high-level description of what you’re building and why, and the coding agent generates a detailed specification. This isn’t about technical stacks or app design. It’s about user journeys, experiences, and what success looks like. Who will use this? What problem does it solve for them? How will they interact with it? What outcomes matter? Think of it as mapping the user experience you want to create, and letting the coding agent flesh out the details. Crucially, this becomes a living artifact that evolves as you learn more about your users and their needs.

2. **Plan**: Now you get technical. In this phase, you provide the coding agent with your desired stack, architecture, and constraints, and the coding agent generates a comprehensive technical plan. If your company standardizes on certain technologies, this is where you say so. If you’re integrating with legacy systems, have compliance requirements, or have performance targets you need to hit … all of that goes here. You can also ask for multiple plan variations to compare and contrast different approaches. If you make your internal docs available to the coding agent, it can integrate your architectural patterns and standards directly into the plan. After all, a coding agent needs to understand the rules of the game before it starts playing.

3. **Tasks**: The coding agent takes the spec and the plan and breaks them down into actual work. It generates small, reviewable chunks that each solve a specific piece of the puzzle. Each task should be something you can implement and test in isolation; this is crucial because it gives the coding agent a way to validate its work and stay on track, almost like a test-driven development process for your AI agent. Instead of “build authentication,” you get concrete tasks like “create a user registration endpoint that validates email format.”

4. **Implement**: Your coding agent tackles the tasks one by one (or in parallel, where applicable). But here’s what’s different: instead of reviewing thousand-line code dumps, you, the developer, review focused changes that solve specific problems. The coding agent knows what it’s supposed to build because the specification told it. It knows how to build it because the plan told it. And it knows exactly what to work on because the task told it.

Crucially, your role isn’t just to steer. It’s to verify. At each phase, you reflect and refine. Does the spec capture what you actually want to build? Does the plan account for real-world constraints? Are there omissions or edge cases the AI missed? The process builds in explicit checkpoints for you to critique what’s been generated, spot gaps, and course correct before moving forward. The AI generates the artifacts; you ensure they’re right.

## How to use Spec Kit in your agentic workflows

Spec Kit works with coding agents like GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI. The key is to use a series of simple commands to steer the coding agent, which then does the hard work of generating the artifacts for you.

Setting it up is straightforward. First, install the `specify` command-line tool. This tool initializes your project and sets up the necessary structure.

```plaintext
uvx --from git+https://github.com/github/spec-kit.git specify init <PROJECT_NAME>
```

Once your project is initialized, use the `/specify` command to provide a high-level prompt, and the coding agent generates the full spec. Focus on the “what” and “why” of your project, not the technical details.

Next, use the `/plan` command to steer the coding agent to create a technical implementation plan. Here, you provide the high-level technical direction, and the coding agent will generate a detailed plan that respects your architecture and constraints.

Finally, use the `/tasks` command to make the coding agent break down the specification and plan into a list of actionable tasks. Your coding agent will then use this list to implement the project requirements.

This structured workflow turns vague prompts into clear intent that coding agents can reliably execute.

But why does this approach succeed where vague prompting fails?

## Why this works

This approach succeeds where “just prompting the AI” fails due to a basic truth about how language models work: they’re exceptional at pattern completion, but not at mind reading. A vague prompt like “add photo sharing to my app” forces the model to guess at  potentially thousands of unstated requirements. The AI will make reasonable assumptions, and some will be wrong (and you often won’t discover which aren’t quite right until deep into your implementation).

By contrast, providing a clear specification up front, along with a technical plan and focused tasks, gives the coding agent more clarity, improving its overall efficacy. Instead of guessing at your needs, it knows what to build, how to build it, and in what sequence.

This is why the approach works across different technology stacks. Whether you’re building in Python, JavaScript, or Go, the fundamental challenge is the same: translating your intent into working code. The specification captures the intent clearly, the plan translates it into technical decisions, the tasks break it into implementable pieces, and your AI coding agent handles the actual coding.

For larger organizations, this solves another critical problem: Where do you put all your requirements around security policies, compliance rules, design system constraints, and integration needs? Often, these things either live in someone’s head, are buried in a wiki that nobody reads, or are scattered across Slack conversations that are impossible to find later.

With Spec Kit, all of that stuff goes in the specification and the plan, where the AI can actually use it. Your security requirements aren’t afterthoughts; they’re baked into the spec from day one. And your design system isn’t something you bolt on later. It’s part of the technical plan that guides implementation.

The iterative nature of this approach is what gives it power. Where traditional development locks you into early decisions, spec-driven makes changing course simple: just update the spec, regenerate the plan, and let the coding agent handle the rest.

## 3 places this approach works really well

Spec-driven development is especially useful in three scenarios:

1. **Greenfield (zero-to-one)**: When you’re starting a new project, it’s tempting to just start coding. But a small amount of upfront work to create a spec and a plan ensures the AI builds what you actually intend, not just a generic solution based on common patterns.
2. **Feature work in existing systems (N-to-N+1)**: This is where spec-driven development is most powerful. Adding features to a complex, existing codebase is hard. By creating a spec for the new feature, you force clarity on how it should interact with the existing system. The plan then encodes the architectural constraints, ensuring the new code feels native to the project instead of a bolted-on addition. This makes ongoing development faster and safer. To make this work, advanced context engineering practices might be needed — we’ll cover those separately.
3. **Legacy modernization**: When you need to rebuild a legacy system, the original intent is often lost to time. With the spec-driven development process offered in Spec Kit, you can capture the essential business logic in a modern spec, design a fresh architecture in the plan, and then let the AI rebuild the system from the ground up, without carrying forward inherited technical debt.

The core benefit is separating the stable “what” from the flexible “how,” enabling iterative development without expensive rewrites. This allows you to build multiple versions and experiment quickly.

## Where we’re headed

We’re moving from “code is the source of truth” to “intent is the source of truth.” With AI the specification becomes the source of truth and determines what gets built.

This isn’t because documentation became more important. It’s because AI makes specifications executable. When your spec turns into working code automatically, it determines what gets built.

Spec Kit is our experiment in making that transition real. We open sourced it because this approach is bigger than any one tool or company. The real innovation is the process. There is more here that we’ll cover soon, specifically around how you can combine spec-driven development practices with context engineering to build more advanced capabilities in your AI toolkit.

And we’d love to hear how it works for you and what we can improve! If you’re building with spec-driven patterns, [share your experience](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/issues) with us. We’re particularly curious about:

- **Making the workflow more engaging and usable**: Reading walls of text can be tedious. How do we make this process genuinely enjoyable?
- **Possible VS Code integrations**: We’re exploring ways to bring this workflow directly into VS Code. What would feel most natural to you?
- **Comparing and diffing multiple implementations**: Iterating and diffing between implementations opens up creative possibilities. What would be most valuable here?
- **Managing specs and tasks at scale in your organization**: Managing lots of Markdown files can get overwhelming. What would help you stay organized and focused?

We’re excited to see you leverage AI to figure out better ways to translate human creativity into working software.

[Get started with Spec Kit >](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)

* * *

- [GitHub Copilot](https://github.blog/tag/github-copilot/)

[Give feedback.](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/1482#) All reactions Comment options # {{title}} Quote reply edited # {{editor}}'s edit {{actor}} deleted this content . # {{editor}}'s edit ### [![](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/247332?s=64&v=4)\ jbaruch](https://github.com/jbaruch) [on Feb 4Feb 4, 2026](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/1482\#discussioncomment-15692154) - | | | --- | | For those looking for alternatives, I've been working on **[Intent Integrity Kit (IIKit)](https://github.com/intent-integrity-chain/kit)** — a fork that extends Spec-Kit with cryptographic verification to prevent a critical failure mode: **circular validation** (where AI modifies tests to match buggy code).
### What's different from Spec-Kit:
1. **Skills instead of Prompts** — IIKit migrates from prompt files to the **skills** format. Why does this matter?
- **Better alignment**: Agents follow skills more reliably than raw prompts
- **Standardized**: Skills are a recognized format across multiple AI coding assistants
- **Better distribution**: Skills are distributed via [Tessl](https://tessl.io/), a proper package registry with versioning, dependencies, and 2000+ tiles for libraries/frameworks
2. **Assertion Integrity** — SHA256 hashing locks your Given/When/Then scenarios before implementation. If tests get modified, implementation is blocked until you go back to the spec. This is a new phase (`/iikit-05-testify`) that generates test specifications from requirements and locks them cryptographically.

3. **Runtime Knowledge via Tessl MCP** — During implementation, IIKit queries **Tessl's library documentation in real-time** via MCP. This means the AI uses _current_ APIs and patterns (React 19, not React 16 from training data). The `/iikit-03-plan` phase auto-discovers and installs relevant tiles for your tech stack.

4. **Cross-Platform** — Full Bash + PowerShell parity, works with Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode.

The core workflow builds on the phased approach Spec-Kit pioneered (constitution → specify → clarify → plan → tasks → implement), with added guardrails to ensure tests actually validate your requirements, not the AI's bugs.
```
Intent ──▶ Spec ──▶ Test ──▶ Code
↑ ↑ ↑
│ │ └── hash verified (no tampering)
│ └─────────── Given/When/Then locked
└─────────────────── clarified until aligned
```
**Links:**
- GitHub: [https://github.com/intent-integrity-chain/kit](https://github.com/intent-integrity-chain/kit)
- Install: `tessl install tessl-labs/intent-integrity-kit`
- Methodology: [https://github.com/intent-integrity-chain/whitepaper](https://github.com/intent-integrity-chain/whitepaper)
- Tessl registry: [https://tessl.io](https://tessl.io/)
Happy to answer questions or hear feedback! | BetaWas this translation helpful? git mv assets/joc-*.gif -> assets/jeo-*.gif (preserve history) - update README.md and README.ko.md prose, headings, CLI examples, and img src paths to match the jeo-code CLI (`jeo`), not the stale joc naming") | last weekJul 3, 2026 | | [changelog](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/changelog "changelog") | [changelog](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/changelog "changelog") | [docs: remove changelog contents, add Joc persona equipment GIFs, and …](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/e3d0cfac6d304c9d2214e7add7824100e25d38a4 "docs: remove changelog contents, add Joc persona equipment GIFs, and attach workflow SVG - Remove all changelog contents: deleted monthly YYYY-MM.md files under changelog/en/ and changelog/ko/, cleared CHANGELOG.md to header only, and cleared the What's New windows in README.md/README.ko.md (verified via changelog.py check). - Joc Persona Equipment: referenced the @../jeo-code Socratic AI agent persona (Joc) and generated 5 glowing, hover-animated pixel-art game item GIFs (Robe of Clarity -> ooo interview, Armor of Lock -> MutationGuard, Boots of Swiftness -> cli-anything execute, Staff of Planning -> spec-kit plan, Carpet of Verification -> ooo/joc ultragoal) under assets/ using god-tibo-imagen + PIL. - Workflow SVG: created and attached assets/workflow.svg showing the 3-step installation flow and the 5-step default operating rail tool flow (ooo -> spec-kit -> cli-anything -> evaluate -> wiki/graphify) with the ooo ralph loop.") | last weekJul 2, 2026 | | [hooks](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/hooks "hooks") | [hooks](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/hooks "hooks") | [Fix Codex config.toml TOML corruption + wire turn-end wiki/graph refresh](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/a367be6668ecf617faf6e789d7ed35a88ea4b57b "Fix Codex config.toml TOML corruption + wire turn-end wiki/graph refresh - Add a defensive TOML integrity guard at every point the guide touches ~/.codex/config.toml (Step 3c ooo MCP, Step 3f semble MCP, Step 3g omx setup): detects a stray root-level `hooks = true|false` scalar that collides with the `[hooks.state.\"...\"]` table Codex itself appends to track hook-trust hashes (symptom: \"cannot extend value of type boolean with a dotted key\" on every codex launch). No-ops on an already-valid file, backs up before writing, and re-homes the removed boolean under `[features].hooks` so its original intent (feature flag) is preserved without occupying the `hooks` table namespace Codex needs. Verified against a synthetic repro of the exact reported error, an existing- [features]-table variant, and idempotent re-runs; also confirmed root- level keys that happen to precede the stray line (e.g. `model`) are no longer swallowed into the new [features] table. - Extend the Knowledge Pipeline hooks so llm-wiki/graphify refresh at turn-end, not only on prompt submit: ingest-prompt.py now treats Stop/AfterAgent/post-turn events as a graph-only refresh (no prompt capture), and Step 6 registers that turn-end hook alongside the existing prompt-submit hook for Claude Code (Stop), Codex CLI (Stop via hooks.json), and Gemini/Antigravity (AfterAgent) — each reusing the already-installed wrapper script. Verified end-to-end against stub agent binaries in a scratch HOME: all six hook registrations land correctly and are idempotent on re-run; ingest-prompt.py's new turn-end branch and the pre-existing prompt-capture branch both still behave correctly under direct stdin simulation.") | 5 days agoJul 7, 2026 | | [python/bin](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/python/bin "This path skips through empty directories") | [python/bin](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/python/bin "This path skips through empty directories") | [Automated commit for workflow wf-1782129847571-347](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/5c0a33b49e99fbad9effc0cd7e992f88bdb0358b "Automated commit for workflow wf-1782129847571-347") | 3 weeks agoJun 22, 2026 | | [scripts](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/scripts "scripts") | [scripts](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/tree/main/scripts "scripts") | [readme: install section first, changelog last (5-date window)](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/7ce116b682ec0125bf91948970d02a008b0cd205 "readme: install section first, changelog last (5-date window) README.md / README.ko.md had `## 🆕 What's New` sitting right after the one-line intro and *before* `## 📦 Installation` — a first-time reader had to scroll past the whole changelog before reaching install steps. - Swapped section order: `## 📦 Installation` (`## 📦 설치`) is now the first content section after the intro; `## 🆕 What's New` is now the last content section, right before the closing \"Buy Me a Coffee\" footer. Korean-only Quick Start / Architecture sections keep their existing position (untouched, out of scope). - `scripts/changelog.py`: WINDOW 3 -> 5, so the README changelog window shows the 5 most recent dates instead of 3. `changelog/README.md` and every `changelog/{en,ko}/*.md` monthly header updated to say \"latest 5 dates\" / \"최신 5개\" to match (was already inconsistent: the doc's own usage example said \"latest-5-date window\" while the code said 3). - The `v2026-07-01` entry had been hand-added directly into the README window without also landing in the monthly changelog files (the actual source of truth) — backfilled `changelog/en/2026-07.md` and `changelog/ko/2026-07.md` from the current README content first, so `sync` didn't drop it. Verified: `python3 scripts/changelog.py sync && python3 scripts/changelog.py check` -> \"OK: README windows match changelog/ monthly files\"; manual double-blank-line sweep on both READMEs to catch swap-seam artifacts (fixed two introduced by the reorder; left pre-existing unrelated ones in README.ko.md's skills table alone).") | last weekJul 1, 2026 | | [.gitattributes](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/.gitattributes ".gitattributes") | [.gitattributes](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/.gitattributes ".gitattributes") | [chore: track \*.gif via Git LFS](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/5c5436ac8da2b6fecc34cf9b9bd78b7f3594c9aa "chore: track *.gif via Git LFS") | 3 weeks agoJun 22, 2026 | | [.gitignore](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/.gitignore ".gitignore") | [.gitignore](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/.gitignore ".gitignore") | [Add /.jeo/ to .gitignore](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/989577261dfcd35732ff4aa385051f8d148e9d93 "Add /.jeo/ to .gitignore") | 2 weeks agoJul 1, 2026 | | [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md "CHANGELOG.md") | [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md "CHANGELOG.md") | [docs: remove changelog contents, add Joc persona equipment GIFs, and …](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/commit/e3d0cfac6d304c9d2214e7add7824100e25d38a4 "docs: remove changelog contents, add Joc persona equipment GIFs, and attach workflow SVG - New references/research-harvesting.md: documents a research-harvesting mode for scraping scientific literature/datasets/citations, modeled on ai4s-research/open-science's skill standard (stdlib-only script, one ```review` fenced JSON block, findings-only contract). - New scripts/research_harvest_gate.py: stdlib-only pre-flight gate that checks robots.txt disallow/crawl-delay and flags known paywalled-publisher domains (heuristic) before harvesting; tested against arxiv.org (real crawl-delay parsed) and sciencedirect.com/link.springer.com (paywall heuristic + unreachable-robots handling). - SKILL.md: add Step 7 (research-harvesting gate), Example 7, best practice #8, new References entries, version bump 1.1 -> 1.2, tags. - SKILL.toon: add step 7 and a research-gate example to the compact index. - [Permalink: 💡 What is Agent Skills?](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#-what-is-agent-skills) A curated collection of 148 agent skills for spec-first, multi-agent LLM workflows — Claude, Gemini, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode, and [jeopi](https://github.com/akillness/jeopi). ## 🎮 Jeo Agent & The Legendary Equipment Set [Permalink: 🎮 Jeo Agent & The Legendary Equipment Set](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#-jeo-agent--the-legendary-equipment-set) `jeo-skills` acts as a legendary equipment set for the `@../jeo-code` Socratic spec-first AI coding agent, **Jeo** (`jeo`). The LLM installer auto-detects your OS and picks the right package manager (`brew` / `snap` / `winget`) and paths (`$HOME` / `$USERPROFILE` / `$XDG_DATA_HOME`) for each tool. ### ✨ Recommended: LLM-driven install (one prompt, all platforms) [Permalink: ✨ Recommended: LLM-driven install (one prompt, all platforms)](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#-recommended-llm-driven-install-one-prompt-all-platforms) Hand the setup prompt to your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, …). It reads the guide, detects your OS, installs the `skills` CLI, adds every skill into the correct per-agent paths, and registers the MCP/shell tools — no manual steps. ``` # Fetch the delegation guide and hand it to your agent curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/akillness/jeo-skills/main/setup-all-skills-prompt.md ``` Or just paste the URL into the agent chat: > Read `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/akillness/jeo-skills/main/setup-all-skills-prompt.md` in full and follow it to install the jeo-skills. The agent runs a **full install by default** (say "core only" or "minimal" to narrow it) and will: - detect macOS / Linux / Windows and select `brew` / `snap` / `winget` \+ the right install paths, - install the `skills` CLI and add skills with correct `-a` agent targeting (no duplicate platform exposure), - register MCP tools (`ooo`, `semble`), shell tooling (`rtk`), and the `oh-my-claudecode` plugin, - **preserve any pre-existing skills** — it only adds or updates, never deletes. * * * ### Manual install (advanced / CI / no-agent) [Permalink: Manual install (advanced / CI / no-agent)](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#manual-install-advanced--ci--no-agent) For scripted or CI environments where no agent is in the loop, run the steps yourself. #### Step 0: Install `skills` CLI [Permalink: Step 0: Install skills CLI](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#step-0-install-skills-cli) ``` npm install -g skills skills --version ``` #### Scope and paths [Permalink: Scope and paths](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#scope-and-paths) The Vercel `skills` CLI installs from GitHub shorthand, full Git URLs, direct skill paths, or local folders: ``` # Project install: writes to the current repo's agent skill directory npx skills add https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills --skill deepinit --skill deep-dive # Global install: available to that agent across projects npx skills add -g https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills --skill deepinit --skill deep-dive # Target specific agents npx skills add -g https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills --skill deepinit --skill deep-dive -a claude-code -a codex -y ``` | OS / shell | Global examples | Project examples | | --- | --- | --- | | macOS / Linux | `$HOME/.claude/skills/`, `$HOME/.codex/skills/`, `$HOME/.gemini/skills/`, `$HOME/.config/opencode/skills/`, `$HOME/.agents/skills/`, `$HOME/.jeopi/agent/skills/`, `$HOME/.jeo/agent/skills/` | `.claude/skills/`, `.agents/skills/`, `.jeopi/skills/`, `.jeo/skills/` | | Windows PowerShell | `$env:USERPROFILE\.claude\skills\`, `$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\skills\`, `$env:USERPROFILE\.gemini\skills\`, `$env:APPDATA\opencode\skills\`, `$env:USERPROFILE\.agents\skills\`, `$env:USERPROFILE\.jeopi\agent\skills\`, `$env:USERPROFILE\.jeo\agent\skills\` | `.claude\skills\`, `.agents\skills\`, `.jeopi\skills\`, `.jeo\skills\` | | Windows Git Bash / WSL2 | `$HOME/.claude/skills/`, `$HOME/.codex/skills/`, `$HOME/.gemini/skills/`, `$HOME/.config/opencode/skills/`, `$HOME/.agents/skills/`, `$HOME/.jeopi/agent/skills/`, `$HOME/.jeo/agent/skills/` | `.claude/skills/`, `.agents/skills/`, `.jeopi/skills/`, `.jeo/skills/` | Project scope is the default and should be committed when the team needs the same skill behavior. Global scope uses `-g` and is better for personal defaults. Agent-specific paths are selected with `-a`; the portable common layer is `.agents/skills/`. #### Choose by platform [Permalink: Choose by platform](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#choose-by-platform) ``` # Claude Code npx skills add https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills \ --skill omc --skill plannotator --skill agentation \ --skill ooo --skill vibe-kanban # Gemini CLI npx skills add https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills \ --skill ohmg --skill ooo --skill vibe-kanban antigravity extensions install https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills # Codex CLI npx skills add https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills \ --skill omx --skill ooo # jeopi — no `-a` id needed: jeopi natively discovers ~/.agents/skills plus the # .claude/.codex/.config/opencode skill dirs, so a global install is enough. npx skills add -g https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills \ --skill deep-research --skill god-tibo-imagen --skill perfectpixel # optional jeopi/jeo-only pin: ~/.jeopi/agent/skills/ (global) or .jeopi/skills/ (project) [Same for .jeo] ``` #### Core tool setup (all platforms) [Permalink: Core tool setup (all platforms)](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#core-tool-setup-all-platforms) ``` # ooo MCP — spec-first control loop pip install "ouroboros-ai[all]" claude mcp add ooo -s user -- ouroboros mcp # Claude Code # Codex: appends [mcp_servers.ooo] to ~/.codex/config.toml via setup-all-skills-prompt.md # (Codex CLI reads TOML, not mcp.json — prior JSON-based registration was a silent no-op) # semble MCP — token-efficient code search claude mcp add semble -s user -- uvx --from "semble[mcp]" semble # rtk — token-optimized shell output # macOS: brew install rtk | Linux: cargo install rtk | Windows: winget install rtk rtk init -g # oh-my-claudecode plugin /plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Yeachan-Heo/oh-my-claudecode /plugin install oh-my-claudecode && setup omc ``` * * * ## MCP server install: `claude mcp add ooo -s user -- ouroboros mcp`. | Phase | Owner | Description | | --- | --- | --- | | Clarify / Spec | `ooo interview` | Interview grounded in live git data (`.ouroboros/interview-context.md`: commits · churn · contributors, regenerated every interview); freeze acceptance criteria before execution | | Plan | `spec-kit` (`/speckit.plan` → `/speckit.tasks`) | Render the reviewable execution plan **from the frozen seed** (one-way seed → plan; installed by default via `OOO_SPEC_KIT=1`) | | Plan / Review | `plannotator` \+ `bmad` | Shape and approve the plan without reopening settled work | | Execute | `cli-anything` (`cli-hub search` → `install` → `launch`) | Drive real software through agent-native CLI harnesses; `--json` output is the evaluate-stage evidence (installed by default via `OOO_CLI_ANYTHING=1`) | | Runtime handoff / Execute | `omc` / `omx` / `ohmg` | Keep runtime-native config and execution in the runtime skill | | Verify / QA | `browser-harness` | Record CDP browser / QA evidence before claiming completion | | Verify UI | `agentation` | Wait for explicit submit, then process UI feedback | | Durable knowledge | `llm-wiki` \+ `graphify` | File significant findings into the wiki and graph | ### plannotator — Visual Plan Review [Permalink: plannotator — Visual Plan Review](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills#plannotator--visual-plan-review) > Keyword: `plan` \| [Docs](https://github.com/akillness/jeo-skills/blob/main/docs/plannotator/README.md) \| [GitHub](https://github.com/backnotprop/plannotator) Browser UI for annotating AI plans. The frontier is now judged by history[] order (the newest step-level entry wins — which forceStatus already appends), and a rolled-back never-completed attempt derives as not-started instead of inheriting an earlier step's boundary as its end. durationTrusted now follows the entry that actually closed the span. Closes #414 Co-authored-by: Claude Fable 5 ") | 2 days agoJul 10, 2026 | | [.storybook](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/tree/main/.storybook ".storybook") | [.storybook](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/tree/main/.storybook ".storybook") | [feat(spec-viewer): rich structured rendering + terminal design refresh (](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/commit/0000ad34dbdf0f7d41411c8152ee639f1a971fa1 "feat(spec-viewer): rich structured rendering + terminal design refresh (#358) * fix: restore native binaries in lockfile so Storybook/Vite build The committed package-lock.json was missing the entire @rollup/rollup-* optional platform tree (and the darwin esbuild binary), so a clean npm install could not build Storybook or the Vite-based webview tooling on any OS — a manifestation of npm's optional-dependency bug (#4828). Regenerated the lockfile so every platform's rollup/esbuild binary is recorded; macOS dev and Linux CI both resolve their binaries now. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * chore(storybook): upgrade 8.6 → 9.1 Ran the official `storybook upgrade` migrator. v9 folds the former addon-essentials (viewport/controls/actions/interactions) and the docs blocks into core, so `@storybook/addon-essentials` and `@storybook/blocks` are dropped and `@storybook/addon-docs` is the only remaining addon. All 169 stories build and the full jest suite stays green. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * feat(spec-viewer): rich, structured rendering of spec documents Render spec/plan/tasks and their artifacts as structured HTML instead of a flat markdown column, all driven by the data already on disk: - Spec: requirement/criteria rows (FR/SC/NFR badges), key-entity rows, acceptance scenarios stacked into Given/When/Then lines. - Plan: Technical Context key/value grid and Constitution Check verdict rows, both collapsed by default. - Tasks: phase headers (MVP/priority chips) and each task merged with what it actually did + the files it touched, from .spec-context.json. - Artifacts: research Decision/Rationale/Alternatives cards, spec-quality checklist as a pass/fail report, contracts; brighter file-tree with light directory/comment coloring. - Tables now honor escaped pipes and clamp ragged rows. - Card hover affordance restored; per-item components stay commentable (the inline \"+\" line affordance), and code blocks/file icons render faithfully in Storybook. Replaced side-stripe accents with subtler background tints. Storybook reorganized under Viewer/Markdown Rendering into Spec / Plan / Tasks / Artifacts, each a Complete (real spec via ?raw) story plus Components. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * feat(spec-viewer): terminal/techy design language Give the viewer a more modern, terminal-native character, applied through the primitives so it stays consistent and theme-adaptive: - Squared corners via the radius tokens (the holdout hardcoded radii now point at the token too) — cascades to buttons, cards, badges, inputs, toasts, modals. - Terminal buttons (shared primitive + footer + install banner): monospace, uppercase, color-flip on hover (outline fills with the accent), no lift/glow. - Terminal cards: border flips to the accent on hover instead of a lift+shadow. - Fixed a task-row hover flicker: the hover bled the background via animated margin/padding, which shifted the row under the cursor; only the background animates now. Adds docs/PRODUCT.md + docs/DESIGN.md (impeccable context: register, the VS-Code -native constraint, the token system, and this direction with its guardrails), plus Storybook Experiments/Techy Buttons & Cards as references. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * wip(spec-viewer): terminal nav, badge & timeline polish (under review) Iterating on the terminal look — still being eyeballed live, not final: - ID badges (FR/SC/phase/decision/checklist) → visible outlined chips (accent border + fill) instead of the faint 10% tint. - Stepper: squared status boxes, roomier spacing, a `❯` prompt + sharp box on the current step; sub-tabs use an accent underline (not a box). - Active highlights + primary buttons use a new --accent-strong (the vivid button blue) so they don't read muted as thin focus-border lines. - Sub-nav row always renders (stable nav height — no jump switching steps). - Branch badge de-purpled to neutral; TOC active item drops its side-stripe for a `❯` + accent treatment. - Content prose bumped a touch larger; labels stay monospace + uppercase. - PHASES activity: drop the per-task time rows (show phase totals + substeps), squared/mono the actor badge. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): address code-review findings - Escape heading titles: phase + user-story titles now go through parseInline like every other captured field, so a `<`/`&` in a heading can't reach #markdown-content innerHTML unescaped (and inline markdown in titles renders). - File-tree coloring: split off a trailing comment before dir-coloring so a `word/` inside a `# comment` no longer gets a nested tree-dir span. - Drop the dead duplicate class checks in the passthrough block (req-row / entity-row / con-row / phase-header / user-story-header are already handled by the wrapComponentLine block that continues). - PhasesCard per-task filter now keys off a real `task` flag on the timeline event model instead of matching the `T001` name shape — a substep named like `t3` is no longer dropped. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): design polish from screenshot review - Sub-headings (h3+) were painted in descriptionForeground (below WCAG AA); now use the readable body token, hierarchy comes from size/weight. - Constitution Check now also recognizes the \"**Name** — note … PASS.\" format (verdict at the end of the sentence), not only \"**Name**: PASS\", so those plans get the PASS/FAIL chips instead of plain bullets. - Inline code gets a deliberate neutral chip (elevated bg + hairline border, squared) instead of an unstyled span picking up an off-theme tint. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * feat(spec-viewer): underline current step, vertical Activity timeline, de-striped callouts - Stepper current step drops the floating outline box for a `❯` + accent underline — the same active treatment the sub-tabs use, so the nav reads as one system. - Activity \"Phases\" is now a vertical timeline: phases stacked on a left spine with a node each, giving substeps room instead of cramped columns. - Callouts (Purpose/Note/Warning/…) and the Template Instructions block lose their left-edge accent stripe for a full hairline border + faint tint; the colored uppercase label carries the type. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): larger, more readable spec typography Body text was tied to the editor font (so it shrank when that was small); it now floors at 16px (matching VS Code's native markdown preview) and scales up from there. Headings grew to a clearer hierarchy (section headings ~24px vs ~18px before), and the tight requirement rows and list items got more line breathing room. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): load the Geist font; scenario cards with tighter steps - The viewer's @font-face used the non-standard format('woff2-variations') hint, which modern Chromium skips — so Geist never loaded and the panel silently fell back to the system sans. Use format('woff2') so the bundled Geist actually renders. - Each acceptance scenario is now its own card with the scenario number as a chip, instead of a bare numbered list item. - The Given/When/Then rows go back to compact spacing (they'd inherited the looser body-list line-height from the readability pass). Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): acceptance scenarios read as flowing sentences again Revert the stacked Given/When/Then layout, the per-scenario card, and the number chip. Each scenario reads as one continuous sentence with the Given/When/Then keywords emphasized inline (blue/amber/green), the way it read before — sentence integrity preserved, no per-clause line breaks. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): more breathing room between paragraphs Bump the gap between body paragraphs so blocks like the user-story body (Why this priority / Independent Test) don't run together. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * docs(screenshots): refresh viewer captures for the redesign Reshoot viewer.png + activity.png against the terminal/Geist redesign, and add create-spec, sidebar, specify, tasks, plan, checklist, research, and stepper captures for the README and docs. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * docs: changelog + README for the rich-rendering viewer redesign Add changelog entries for rich structured spec rendering and the refreshed, more-readable viewer; update the README viewer blurb to describe the rich rendering and fix the Activity \"Phases\" description (now a vertical timeline). Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) * fix(spec-viewer): keep the Constitution Check heading for the table form preprocessConstitution stripped the `## Constitution Check` heading unconditionally but only emitted the collapsible replacement for the bullet form, so a table-form Constitution Check lost its section header. Defer the heading and restore it when no verdict rows are collected. Committed write-only ingestion credential. - New speckit.telemetry setting (default true) with a privacy-explaining description. - Per-spec correlation via a random telemetryInstanceId persisted in .spec-context.json (added to types + schema), lazily backfilled. - Seven events wired at real dispatch sites: extension.activated (+ beta snapshot), provider.selected, phase.dispatched (sidebar + viewer paths), spec.created, spec.completed, spec.archived, workflow.selected (user-defined workflows reported as the literal \"custom\"). - Tests cover the dual gate, empty-connection-string no-op, instanceId generate/persist/reuse, and beta-snapshot assembly; mock added for @vscode/extension-telemetry. - README Telemetry section + CHANGELOG entry. Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 * refactor: address code-review findings (#129) - Sanitize phase.dispatched 'phase' to built-in steps or 'custom' (privacy: custom-workflow step names are user-authored, never sent verbatim) - Coerce on-disk 'profile' to the standard/turbo enum before reporting it (free-text profile field could otherwise leak via phase.dispatched) - Map legacy 'default' workflow name to the built-in 'speckit' id - Backfill telemetryInstanceId via updateSpecContext (re-read-then-set) so a concurrent skill/hook write isn't clobbered - Fire extension.activated after the beta-settings migration; funnel the three former tri-state flags through coerceLegacyBoolean in the snapshot - Derive spec.created profile from the resolved command so project-default-turbo creations report 'turbo' - Tie built-in phase set to WorkflowSteps constants Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 * fix(telemetry): allow-list templateProfile before reporting (#129) A user can hand-set an arbitrary speckit.companion.templateProfile in settings.json; coerce to standard/turbo/off (default off) so the beta snapshot never sends a verbatim free-text value. + test. Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 --------- Co-authored-by: Claude Fable 5 ") [#289](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/pull/289) [)](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/commit/07d52e7a216b2fd8960a9738b38103661aba0efc "feat(telemetry): opt-in PII-free AI-provider-adoption telemetry (#289) * feat(telemetry): opt-in PII-free provider-adoption telemetry (#129) Add an anonymous, opt-in telemetry pipeline backed by Application Insights via @vscode/extension-telemetry, firing seven PII-free events about AI provider / pipeline-profile / beta-flag adoption and the spec lifecycle. - New TelemetryService (src/core/telemetry.ts) wrapping TelemetryReporter, gated on both speckit.telemetry and VS Code global telemetry; fails closed on an empty connection string. Committed write-only ingestion credential. - New speckit.telemetry setting (default true) with a privacy-explaining description. - Per-spec correlation via a random telemetryInstanceId persisted in .spec-context.json (added to types + schema), lazily backfilled. - Seven events wired at real dispatch sites: extension.activated (+ beta snapshot), provider.selected, phase.dispatched (sidebar + viewer paths), spec.created, spec.completed, spec.archived, workflow.selected (user-defined workflows reported as the literal \"custom\"). - Tests cover the dual gate, empty-connection-string no-op, instanceId generate/persist/reuse, and beta-snapshot assembly; mock added for @vscode/extension-telemetry. - README Telemetry section + CHANGELOG entry. Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 * refactor: address code-review findings (#129) - Sanitize phase.dispatched 'phase' to built-in steps or 'custom' (privacy: custom-workflow step names are user-authored, never sent verbatim) - Coerce on-disk 'profile' to the standard/turbo enum before reporting it (free-text profile field could otherwise leak via phase.dispatched) - Map legacy 'default' workflow name to the built-in 'speckit' id - Backfill telemetryInstanceId via updateSpecContext (re-read-then-set) so a concurrent skill/hook write isn't clobbered - Fire extension.activated after the beta-settings migration; funnel the three former tri-state flags through coerceLegacyBoolean in the snapshot - Derive spec.created profile from the resolved command so project-default-turbo creations report 'turbo' - Tie built-in phase set to WorkflowSteps constants Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 * fix(telemetry): allow-list templateProfile before reporting (#129) A user can hand-set an arbitrary speckit.companion.templateProfile in settings.json; coerce to standard/turbo/off (default off) so the beta snapshot never sends a verbatim free-text value. + test. dow…](https://github.com/alfredoperez/speckit-companion/commit/0f78589c1fde7d9d9640aef2766cae0f14336502 "refactor: structural cleanup — 4 rounds, patterns named, ~700 LOC down, +62 tests (#191) * docs: align with reality + add prevention test (refactor Phase 0) The architecture docs had drifted material distances from the codebase: - architecture.md claimed 5 providers; reality is 8 - architecture.md listed dead files (navigation.ts, scratchpad.ts, state.ts) and omitted everything added during the Preact migration - how-it-works.md duplicated a giant file tree that had its own staleness - how-it-works.md referenced src/features/workflow-editor/workflowEditorProvider.ts which has never existed (the file is workflowEditorCommands.ts) - CLAUDE.md hard-coded the same stale 5-provider list at the top Fixes: - architecture.md rewritten responsibility-first. No exhaustive file trees; each section names the files that are structurally important and points at ls for the rest. Every *Provider.ts is named at least once so the docs- consistency test can verify it. - how-it-works.md trimmed: removed the giant duplicated project-structure block in favor of a pointer to architecture.md, fixed all five broken file paths, updated provider count + Mermaid diagram to reflect the real 8. - CLAUDE.md no longer enumerates providers; references the README matrix. - README provider matrix gets a `` marker. Prevention layer (the structural fix): - tests/integration/docs-consistency.test.ts runs on every `npm test`. It asserts (1) package.json aiProvider enum count == README matrix column count, (2) every *Provider.ts under src/ai-providers/ is named in architecture.md, (3) every backticked .ts/.tsx/.css/.json/.md path in the three audited docs resolves on disk. Failures name the specific offender. This is Phase 0 of the structural-cleanup refactor; see docs/refactor-plan.md for the full plan (Phases 1-6 + 8). Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * refactor(ai-providers): extract CliTerminalProvider base (refactor Phase 1) The six terminal-based CLI providers were each independently reimplementing the same dispatch dance: ensure-installed → write temp prompt file → build shell line → create terminal → waitForShellReady → sendText → schedule temp-file cleanup. Bug fixes had to be applied six times; a new provider was a ~150-LOC copy-paste with five constants changed. New shape: CliTerminalProvider (abstract base) - owns ensureInstalled / temp-file / terminal lifecycle / cleanup - default prepareDispatch() handles the \" -p \" pattern - hooks: cliBinary, cliPathSettingKey, installHint, defaultTerminalTitle, headlessTerminalName, logPrefix, preprocessPrompt(), cliPromptFlag(), and prepareDispatch() for providers that need a full override Pattern A — direct dispatch (collapse to ~25 LOC each): QwenCliProvider: 161 → 25 LOC (no overrides) OpenCodeProvider: 132 → 23 LOC (no overrides) CopilotCliProvider: 167 → 29 LOC (overrides preprocessPrompt to strip /) Pattern B — system-prompt split (Claude): ClaudeCodeProvider: 271 → 159 LOC (overrides prepareDispatch + executeSlashCommand; keeps renameTerminal + createPermissionTerminal helpers) Pattern C — codex exec piped streaming + skill-template resolution: CodexCliProvider: 260 → 107 LOC (overrides prepareDispatch and owns skill resolution; everything else inherited) Outside scope: GeminiCliProvider stays as-is. Gemini's interactive-CLI pattern (start gemini, wait initDelay, sendText prompt without Enter, then sendText Enter) doesn't fit the prompt-file dispatch mold. Forcing it through would require enough hooks to defeat the abstraction. Net delta across the 6 provider files: −379 LOC (1151 → 772 including the new 268-LOC base). The bigger structural win: provider #9 onward is now ~25 LOC of constants, not 150 LOC of copy-paste. Docs: architecture.md updated to describe the new base/hook pattern; the docs-consistency test (Phase 0) confirms cliTerminalProvider.ts is named. All 698 tests pass, including the 112 ai-providers tests. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * refactor(ai-providers): validate PROVIDER_PATHS at module load (refactor Phase 2) The 8 entries in PROVIDER_PATHS are typed at compile time, but the TypeScript shape can't catch the runtime failure modes that have actually surfaced: - autoApproveFlag without a trailing space silently concatenates into the next CLI arg. This is the failure pattern behind the feedback_provider_rename_breaks_settings memory. - commandFormat typo (e.g. \"dotted\") routes commands as-is and the user sees their CLI reject every speckit invocation with no clear cause. - quickPickIcon without `$(...)` codicon syntax renders blank. - steeringDir set with steeringPattern empty silently enumerates nothing. - Empty displayName produces a blank QuickPick row. New src/ai-providers/providerRegistry.ts exposes: - validateProviderConfig(type, config) — accumulates field errors and throws ProviderRegistryError naming every offender at once. - validateProviderRegistry(record) — walks all entries; called once at module-load from aiProvider.ts. The exported PROVIDER_PATHS binding is now the post-validation object; the raw literal is named _PROVIDER_PATHS_RAW and never accessible. Any misconfiguration trips activation, not first dispatch. 9 new unit tests cover each rule + an accumulation case + a live smoke check that walks every shipped config. All 707 tests pass. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * refactor(spec-viewer): extract PanelStateComputer for pure derivation (refactor Phase 3) specViewerProvider.ts had three render paths — updateContent (full HTML regen), sendContentUpdateMessage (tab click), and refreshContextIfDisplaying (viewer-state poke) — each independently doing the same derivation: stepHistory from history[], stepBadges with the specify→spec alias, phases, task completion %, the 4-branch specStatus ladder, badgeText, created/ lastUpdated dates, the core/related partition, and the approve-footer logic. The duplication was the root of the \"updateContent is 227 lines\" smell — most of those lines were derivation mixed with I/O. New src/features/spec-viewer/panelStateComputer.ts owns: - resolveDisplayDocument() — pure cascading fallback for full-render - resolveTabClickDocument() — pure tab-click resolution (no fallback) - resolveSpecStatus() — the 4-branch ladder, in one place - computeApproveFooter() — approve-button state derivation - mapStepHistoryToTabKeys() — preserves completed entries when implement aliases onto the tasks tab - deriveStepBadgesWithAlias() — per-step badge with specify→spec copy - computePanelDerivedState() — the integration: one call, full result No `vscode` import. No filesystem. Pure functions over typed inputs. specViewerProvider.ts: - 1110 → 970 LOC (−140) - updateContent() 227 LOC of mixed concerns → ~75 LOC of read → compute → set HTML, with the I/O path explicit - sendContentUpdateMessage() similar shape, same computer - Local mapStepHistoryKeys / deriveStepBadgesWithAlias helpers removed (identical to the exported versions) - Two new I/O helpers extracted: readDocumentContent(), readTasksContent() — both render paths share them 22 new unit tests cover every public function in panelStateComputer with no jest.mock(vscode) — if a future change leaks I/O back in, the test setup will demand a mock and the abstraction breach will be obvious. All 729 tests pass (707 → 729). Net diff: +300 panelStateComputer + 22 tests, −140 specViewerProvider. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * refactor(spec-viewer): typed dispatch map + queue class for messageHandlers (Phase 4) Three structural changes to messageHandlers.ts; line-count delta is small (1001 → 994) because typed boilerplate replaces ad-hoc switch, but the shape change is real. 1. **Typed dispatch map** replaces the 140-line switch ladder. type Handler = ( msg: Extract, specDirectory: string, deps: MessageHandlerDependencies, ) => Promise; const handlers: { [K in MessageType]: Handler } = { ... }; The map type forces every message variant to have an adapter at compile time — adding a new ViewerToExtensionMessage variant fails the build until an entry is added. Manual `as cast` field extraction is gone; each adapter's `msg` is narrowed to its specific variant. Footer actions get the same treatment: FOOTER_ACTION_HANDLERS is a sub-dispatch keyed on FooterActionIds. 2. **handleClarify's three duplicate loops collapsed.** The customCommands loop, the workflow-commands loop, and the optional-command fallback each independently built the same prompt the same way. Now they share `EnhancementCommand` (the normalised shape), `matchesCommand` (the button-vs-implicit-step matcher), and `dispatchEnhancement` (the prompt-build + executeInTerminal call). Each loop is 4 lines. 3. **CommentMutationQueue class** replaces the module-scope `commentWriteQueues = new Map>()`. Same semantics (per-spec serial queue, self-cleaning Map, error chaining via `.then(run, run)`) but now encapsulated — no exported handle for anything else to mutate, lifecycle is explicit in the class. No behaviour change. All 729 tests pass, including the 5-test messageHandlers suite which exercises the dispatch paths. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * docs: close out structural-cleanup refactor (Phase 8) Phases 0-4 are shipped on this branch. Phases 5 and 6 are deferred — both are spec-viewer rewrites that need visual verification of the most-touched UI surface, which can't be done safely from an autonomous session: - Phase 5 (webview rewrite, est. -900 LOC): converting markdown/renderer.ts from HTML-string to JSX, then deleting modal.ts/toc.ts/actions.ts/ editor/inlineEditor.ts/editor/refinements.ts. The single Jest test that covers the renderer is testing HTML output; once the renderer returns JSX, that test has to be rewritten. Storybook stories aren't in `npm test`. - Phase 6 (FooterActions.tsx split): the component has 18 stories but no Jest unit test. The existing footerActions.spec.ts tests the extension- side catalog, not the Preact component. Splitting touches every step transition with no automated regression guard. Both stay in the plan. They need a session with the Extension Development Host open for visual verification, or a Storybook visual-regression CI step landed first. Treat them as /ultrareview-style follow-ups. Refactor closeout state: - 729 tests pass (697 before this work + 32 new: 22 panelStateComputer, 9 providerRegistry, plus inline cases) - 61 test suites (was 58) - `npm test` enforces the docs-consistency invariants forever - net production-code LOC is down; net total +/- is dominated by the new pure-derivation modules and their tests Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * docs(refactor-plan): add round-2 plan (reusability + safe Phase 5/6 landing) Two parallel audits of the post-Phase-0-4 state surfaced different findings than round 1. The big-file watchlist is mostly handled; the remaining wins are architectural boundaries and reusability extracts. Headline insight from the webview review: ship the FooterActions split WITH a Jest test as the first step (Phase 5a). That gives every subsequent imperative-layer kill (5b, 5c, 5d) a regression guard, so the \"webview rewrite needs visual verification\" risk drops from mega-PR to 4 small safe PRs. Headline insight from the backend review: bidirectional coupling between src/features/specs/ and src/features/spec-viewer/ is the biggest remaining structural smell. specExplorerProvider imports isStepCompleted from spec-viewer/stateDerivation — sidebar shouldn't depend on viewer. Move the pure queries down a layer, make the import one-way. Round-2 phases (in docs/refactor-plan.md): 9 — break specs ↔ spec-viewer coupling 10 — extract specCommandRegistry + lift TypedDispatcher to core/utils/ 11 — investigate / delete specContextManager.ts 12 — extract panelLifecycle from specViewerProvider 5a — split FooterActions + Jest test (safety net) 5b — replace modal.ts with 5c — declarative wrappers for InlineEditor + InlineComment 5d — with post-effects in useEffect 13 — useDispatch() hook for the 41 vscode.postMessage callsites Plus an explicit \"we're not doing this\" list so future reviews don't re-flag deliberately-untouched code (CommentMutationQueue stays spec-viewer-scoped, BaseManager stays, etc.). Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * docs(refactor-plan): add round-3 state-handling phases State audit on the post-Round-2 codebase surfaced four issues. Two are genuine fragility, two are sprawl that hurts \"ease of update\": 🚨 Context-key sprawl: 16 setContext call sites with no coordination, no error handling, no reset mechanism. allCollapsed gets stuck; filter + tree provider race on noFilterMatch. 🚨 Spec status derived in 3 shapes: direct ctx.status reads, ad-hoc boolean checks scattered through specExplorer + stateDerivation, and the resolveSpecStatus() ladder added in Phase 3. Adding a new status today touches 6-8 files at non-obvious locations. 🟡 Webview message-ordering: viewerStateUpdated does shallow spread over potentially-null navState.value. Defensive checks downstream paper it over, but the gap is real. 🟡 lastFeatureCtx never invalidated on file deletion or read failure. Step-completion notifier can fire on bogus deltas after transient read fails. 🟢 Most state is fine — single canonical writer for spec context, derived values computed not stored, PanelInstance is clean, signals are well-scoped. Round 3 plan added: 14 — ContextKeyManager class (consolidate 16 writers) 15 — Single getSpecStatus() entry point 16 — SpecsSidebarState class (filter + sort + collapse unified) 17 — Webview state-init fix + lastFeatureCtx guards All low-risk: each phase consolidates already-working logic behind one entry point. No new behaviour. ~1.5 days total. Updated execution ordering in refactor-plan.md: Backend (9-12) → State (14-17) → Webview (5a-5d, 13) Each block independently stoppable. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 * docs(refactor-plan): add round-4 component library phases A library audit asked \"does this repo have an easy-to-use and extendable component library?\" Honest answer: partial. Good foundations (Button, UndoToast, useInlineConfirm, comprehensive tokens.css) but undermined by: - 19+ raw

web.description: Using the spec kit I create a spec and implement it. What is the process when I now have a change request. Spec driven development would seem to indicate ...

[github](https://github.com/github)/ **[spec-kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)** Public - [Notifications](https://github.com/login?return_to=%2Fgithub%2Fspec-kit) You must be signed in to change notification settings - [Fork\\ 10.6k](https://github.com/login?return_to=%2Fgithub%2Fspec-kit) - [Star\\ 120k](https://github.com/login?return_to=%2Fgithub%2Fspec-kit) # Evolving specs \#152 [Ian1971](https://github.com/Ian1971) started this conversation in [General](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/categories/general) [Evolving specs](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152#top)#152 [![@Ian1971](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/318793?s=40&v=4)\\ Ian1971](https://github.com/Ian1971) on Sep 10, 2025Sep 10, 2025· 40 comments · 83 replies [Return to top](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152#top) Discussion options - Copy link - Copy Markdown # {{title}} Quote reply ## [![](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/318793?s=64&v=4)\ Ian1971](https://github.com/Ian1971) [on Sep 10, 2025Sep 10, 2025](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152\#discussion-8877658) Original comment in English - Translate to English | | | --- | | Using the spec kit I create a spec and implement it.
What is the process when I now have a change request. Spec driven development would seem to indicate that the spec is the source of truth for the system, but spec kit leads me to create a new spec with the variation. That doesn't seem to be in keeping with spec driven development, as now to know what the system does I need to read both specs.
Or should I be getting the AI to update the master spec as well? | BetaWas this translation helpful? [Give feedback.](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152#) 71You must be logged in to vote All reactions - ![+1](https://github.githubassets.com/assets/1f44d-41cb66fe1e22.png)24 ## Replies: 40 comments · 83 replies Comment options - Copy link - Copy Markdown # {{title}} Quote reply ### [![](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/793195?s=64&v=4)\ simongartz](https://github.com/simongartz) [on Sep 10, 2025Sep 10, 2025](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152\#discussioncomment-14367349) Original comment in English - Translate to English | | | --- | | You should not change the master spec, if you create a feature you just add the spec with the new feature. If you change you say that you change that in the new spec.
Ex.
spec1 - Todo list with local storage
spec2 - Add login
spec3 - Replace file storage with option to use cloud storage - This one replaces the implementation of the local storage with a cloud storage, to comply with the change request. | BetaWas this translation helpful? [Give feedback.](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152#) 7You must be logged in to vote All reactions - ![+1](https://github.githubassets.com/assets/1f44d-41cb66fe1e22.png)3 16 replies Show 11 previous replies [![@robertDouglass](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/57047?s=60&v=4)](https://github.com/robertDouglass) Comment options - Copy link - Copy Markdown # {{title}} Quote reply #### [robertDouglass](https://github.com/robertDouglass) [on Nov 13, 2025Nov 13, 2025](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152\#discussioncomment-14963062) Original comment in English - Translate to English | | | --- | | [@ed4becky](https://github.com/ed4becky) in my fork - spec kitty - the spec.md is the delta from the existing code (the ground truth) and the requested feature. There's a new spec.md per feature and they can be looked at in historical order, but the only source of ground truth is THE CODE ITSELF. LLMs are super good at reading code and deriving the truth from it. Describing the code to an LLM is a fool's errand (IMO), so the spec.md in spec kitty is the path forward. LLM just has to study the code long enough to really understand how to achieve that delta, which is what the plan phase and research phase are for. | BetaWas this translation helpful? [Give feedback.](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152#) All reactions - ![heart](https://github.githubassets.com/assets/2764-982dc91ea48a.png)5 [![@rosmur](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/89332196?s=60&v=4)](https://github.com/rosmur) Comment options - Copy link - Copy Markdown # {{title}} Quote reply #### [rosmur](https://github.com/rosmur) [on Nov 13, 2025Nov 13, 2025](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152\#discussioncomment-14963454) Original comment in English - Translate to English | | | --- | | [@robertDouglass](https://github.com/robertDouglass) completely disagree with that line of reasoning. The human needs to be the primary entity responsible for the code. Having the code be the source of truth (i.e. current way of doing things) is inherently intractable as no matter how good you are in reading code, it becomes an endeavor in any meaningfully side production codebase. English language docs with diagrams etc are far more readable (as natural language is... natural.)
Specs/intent have always been the initiation point. In many industries, they are the source if truth. We can now have this in software. AI empowers us to switch to having specs as the truth source in a tractable way by having bidirectional syncing in an automated fashion. Something that was impossible before (and thus a common failure mode for specs going outnofndate).

{{editor}}'s edit {{actor}} deleted this content . # {{editor}}'s edit #### [cuyler-at-scribe](https://github.com/cuyler-at-scribe) [on Jan 16Jan 17, 2026](https://github.com/github/spec-kit/discussions/152\#discussioncomment-15522931) - | | | --- | | [@robertDouglass](https://github.com/robertDouglass) "source of truth" seems _(ironically)_ like a vague phrase that's creating ambiguity here.
It begs the question:
A source of **_which_** truth? The truth... _about what_?
The code can serve as a "source of truth for what **is actually** being done" _(except for circumstances where environmental circumstances/state create ambiguity, e.g. Javascript running on varying browser versions)_...
... but it isn't particularly suitable as a "source of truth for **was meant** to be done" _(unless you want to be pedantic and get really extreme with insanely-detailed comments and test descriptions, which would admittedly be a pretty interesting/novel TDD-inspired way of doing SDD)_.
Conflating the two makes sense only in a hypothetical world we don't live in, where "bugs don't exist". You're basically saying "whatever we've been doing is always what we meant to do".
The "spec" and "code" **BOTH** have potential deltas from what was actually communicated _(which itself has a potential delta from what was actually intended by stakeholders)_. Either/or/both can be broken at any given time. Your spec may not correctly/completely describe the problem or the intended solution. Your code may not correctly implement the spec. Or your code may be a slightly-incorrect implementation of a slightly-incorrect/incomplete spec.
This sorta gets into the sometimes-blurry distinction between "unexpected behavior" _(very explicitly a "bug")_ versus "undesired behavior" _(often not a "bug" per-se, perhaps something more akin to an "area for improvement")_. If a PM "gets what they asked for", but it turns out that "what they asked for" isn't really "what they wanted", it's hard to really call this a "bug" _(even though in everyday conversation, we go along with the colliqual terminology because a PM often isn't in a position to recognize the difference, and even if they did understand the difference, they probably wouldn't care anyway)_. For the purpose of "specifications as code", it's very important to be able to distinguish between _(and correct separately)_ a "wrong spec" versus a "wrong implementation of the right spec" _(and vice-versa, "right answer to the wrong question")_.
**Side note:** IMO it's important for both "spec" and "code" to have a 2-way binding, where changes in either can update the other _(and both are always kept in sync)_. Although it's normal in SDD to adjust the spec to generate code, it's sometimes simplest and most efficient to fix a bug in code and then allow the LLM to infer to intent retroactively via looking at the diff and taking it into context. | BetaWas this translation helpful? The human needs to be the primary entity responsible for the code. Having the code be the source of truth (i.e. current way of doing things) is inherently intractable as no matter how good you are in reading code, it becomes an endeavor in any meaningfully side production codebase. English language docs with diagrams etc are far more readable (as natural language is... natural.)
>
> Specs/intent have always been the initiation point. In many industries, they are the source if truth. We can now have this in software. AI empowers us to switch to having specs as the truth source in a tractable way by having bidirectional syncing in an automated fashion. Something that was impossible before (and thus a common failure mode for specs going outnofndate).
>
> Now the only thing remaining is engineering the tooling solution tondo this in an ergonomic, reliable and auditable manner.
[@robertDouglass](https://github.com/robertDouglass) nailed it. The _code-is-truth_ position has always been due to tooling limitations, not a philosophical preference. Nobody wants to reverse-engineer the intent from the the code; we just accepted it because specs often went stale.
What has changed isn't only that AI now can read code, it#s that AI can _keep specs and code in sync bidrirectionally_ which means that the historical failure mode of specs getting outdated fast, is not an engineering problem.
But I'd push this even one step further: living specs don't just solve the spec-drift problem, they change the economics of how agents consume context.
An agent which has to explore the production codebase to decide what to build next, reverse-engineers intent from artifacts at high token cost. By handing it a structured up-to-date spec instead, it'll get the same understanding from much less context at much higher signal density.
So the case I want to make is that living specs are a more efficient interface between humans and agents than code is. They're heigher signal-per-token for both, humans and agents.
The remaining engineering challenge is exactly what you said: making sync ergonomic, reliable and auditable. So, talking about version history, conflict resolution, provenance tracking (who changed what? who's agent? which human?) and a governance model that keeps humans in control without slowing things down. And this can't live inside git push/pull workflows because specs need to sync ahead of commits and in real time in a multi-human-agent team. While Git is for code artifacts, specs need their own sync layer. | BetaWas this translation helpful? I'm sorry I'm late to the party - I'd like to chime in with some of my own thoughts around this issue:
One way of thinking about all of this stuff is that the spec or specs are really the source code for the implementation. Something that Sean Grove said (paraphrasing) at the AI Engineer Summit was around how we spend all this time versioning the source code that today is generated from agents, but yet we don't really do any version control management, updating, ensuring that it's consistent as the source of truth of the specs, which seems odd. It would be like if we spent all of our time carefully versioning the binaries produced by our compilers and throwing away the source code.
So it becomes really important, not so much is there a single master spec, a set of feature specs or anything else. It's that those things must be kept in sync with the source code. And that requires a certain amount of discipline to make it effective. I don't think that for any reasonable size project that we could have just one spec. I think there is some kind of natural hierarchy that evolves in your projects.
What I have taken to doing recently is writing a spec for a feature that is more of a unified spec. Both the motivations as well as the implementation details and the research and other things are captured inside of a single document that I can iterate with the AI. But then what I do is I spend cycles in the agentic loop to close the loop between the code that was actually written and the spec because these things naturally diverge as it's pretty rare for the agent to one-shot the code and then call it a day. Oftentimes there's follow-ups and other things, right? Because you underspecified something, there's a bug, etc. And those changes ultimately become material. So unfortunately right now it's on you to fold those things back into the spec or ask the agent. to fold those changes and updates back into the spec. | BetaWas this translation helpful? I started to evaluate Spec-Kit this week, and I would like to understand the vision on how leveraging the current model will scale overtime in projects where there are multiple iterations.
I've been reasearching, evaluating different Spec Driven Development tools and leveraging them to build greenfield applications.
The way I'm using Spec Driven Development so far which is working for me is:
- Equivalent of the constitution.md, but split into:
- Company/Team specific steering: e.g: Coding conventions, tech convention
- Project specific steering
- Features Spec: Here I'm currently breaking into product features. e.g: One per microservice / component
If we focus on the features spec: The way I'm using is very different than SpecKit is designed to work. The model I'm using works extremely well, however it does have limitations for teams who are developing multiple feature branches in parallel. It's not an issue for me at the moment though.
The way SpecKit handles features, by aligning with feature branches is great for incremental work, but it seems to go against the Spec Driven Development principles (at least based on my understanding).
I see the specifications being long lived specs as the source of truth. It should represent the target state rather than the incremental changes. Or maybe we should decouple the requirement/design from the tasks.
I fully agree with [@simongartz](https://github.com/simongartz) , the middle ground would be a command to "compact" multiple "specs" into "target state specs".
But then I wonder if the concept of a "spec" should be repurposed to represent the "target compacted state", and what is defined as "spec" today, becomes the "spec change".
**A very simple use case:**
For a given microservice which implements a REST API, which have a contract for create\_customer(name, dob). If the feature is already released and now I want to add a new optional attribute called country, I would then invoke: "/spec-change add attribute called country to create\_customer in customer-microservices spec".
The question then is: Should the flow be:
- update the "target state spec" and the "change-spec" (the current spec concept) at the same time, which allows the developer to review both before moving to the next step; OR
- Handle the incremental specs as-is and have a separate process to compact the specs at the end?
I prefer the first option, as it gives more control to the developer and ability to review consistency in near realtime, rather than compacting it at the end. I prefer to interact with target state and review incremental plan, rather than the other way around.
Keen to get others' views on this. | BetaWas this translation helpful? The discussion seems to hinge on whether the whole system should live under a single spec subdirectory, or whether it should be broken down into separate capabilities — each with its own plan, spec, and data model.
Option A – single application spec:
```
specs/
└── 001-application/
├── plan.md
├── spec.md
├── data-model.md
...
```
Option B – capability-based specs:
```
specs/
├── 001-foundation/
│ ├── plan.md
│ ├── spec.md
│ └── data-model.md
├── 002-capability-a/
│ ├── plan.md
│ ├── spec.md
│ └── contracts/
│ └── openapi.yaml
├── 003-capability-b/
│ ├── plan.md
│ ├── spec.md
│ └── data-model.md
└── master-capability-spec.md
```
I’d love to hear from the maintainers or other users which pattern aligns better with SpecKit’s intended workflow for complex, multi-feature systems. | BetaWas this translation helpful?
